input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for digital twin research and validation. The **physical twin** consists of:
- **Core Components**:
  - A **gantry crane mechanism** (likely including a **bridge structure**, **hoist**, and **trolley** for movement along X/Y axes).
  - An **embedded controller** (hardware/software system) responsible for real-time motion control, sensor interfacing, and actuation.
- **Operational Domain**:
  - **Industrial automation/robotics** (scalable for manufacturing, logistics, or research applications).
  - **Kinematic focus**: Positioning, trajectory planning, and dynamic behavior modeling.
- **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
  - **Bi-directional data flow**: Sensors (e.g., encoders, IMUs) feed real-time operational data to the digital twin, while the twin’s services (e.g., optimized control commands) are deployed back to the physical system.
  - **Reproducibility**: Open-source hardware/software stack with no proprietary dependencies, enabling easy replication.
  - **Validation use cases**: Previously tested for **continuous validation methods**, **fault detection**, and **system evolution notation (DarTwin)** in prior publications ([4]–[7]).
  - **Scalability**: Designed as a modular exemplar for broader digital twin research (e.g., synchronization, data streaming, or service optimization).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin** provides **real-time and post-processing services** categorized by function, target users, and technical capabilities:

1. **Optimal Control Services**:
   - **Type**: Closed-loop optimization.
   - **Capability**: Computes **energy-efficient trajectories** or **dynamic compensation** for the crane’s kinematic model, reducing power consumption or improving precision.
   - **Target Users**: Researchers/developers optimizing control algorithms or energy management.

2. **Historical Data Logging**:
   - **Type**: Data acquisition and storage.
   - **Capability**: Logs **sensor data streams** (e.g., joint angles, velocities, forces) and **control commands** at high temporal resolution (unspecified but implied to be ≥100Hz for kinematic systems).
   - **Storage**: Likely structured in a **time-series database** (e.g., InfluxDB or custom CSV/JSON formats) for post-analysis.
   - **Target Users**: Fault diagnosis, performance benchmarking, or machine learning training.

3. **Continuous Validation**:
   - **Type**: Real-time monitoring.
   - **Capability**: Implements **kinematic discrepancy detection** by comparing predicted (digital twin) vs. actual (sensor) states, enabling **proactive fault detection** (e.g., misalignment, wear).
   - **Methodology**: Validated via **residual analysis** or **model predictive control (MPC)** frameworks ([4], [5]).
   - **Target Users**: Maintenance engineers or system operators.

4. **Data Visualization**:
   - **Type**: User interface (UI) and analytics.
   - **Capability**: Renders **3D CAD models** (e.g., via **Blender** or **Python-based visualization tools**) with overlaid **real-time kinematic data** (e.g., trajectory plots, force distributions).
   - **Integration**: Likely uses **Python (Matplotlib/Plotly)** or **web-based dashboards** (e.g., Dash or Tableau) for interactive exploration.
   - **Target Users**: Educators, researchers, or end-users requiring intuitive system monitoring.

5. **System Evolution Notation (DarTwin)**:
   - **Type**: Metadata management.
   - **Capability**: Tracks **system modifications** (e.g., hardware upgrades, software patches) in a **versioned ontology** ([7]), enabling traceability of changes across physical/digital twins.
   - **Target Users**: Digital twin architects or system integrators.

**Service Architecture**:
- **Decoupled design**: Services operate as **microservices** or **modular Python scripts**, allowing independent deployment (e.g., control optimization via ROS or standalone Python).
- **Data Protocols**: Likely uses **ROS (Robot Operating System)** or **MQTT** for real-time sensor/actuator communication.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** is implemented using an **open-source, modular toolchain** with the following **specific technologies and enablers**:

1. **Simulation & Modeling**:
   - **CAD Modeling**: **FreeCAD** or **Blender** for 3D geometry (unspecified but implied for visualization).
   - **Kinematic Modeling**: **Python (SymPy, SciPy)** or **ROS (Robotics Toolbox)** for dynamic equations (e.g., forward/inverse kinematics).
   - **Simulation Engine**: **Gazebo** or **V-REP** (unspecified but referenced in prior digital twin research) for virtual prototyping.

2. **Data Acquisition & Processing**:
   - **Sensors**: **Encoders**, **IMUs**, or **force sensors** (unspecified but standard for kinematic systems).
   - **Data Logging**: **Python (Pandas)** or **ROS bags** for raw data storage.
   - **Streaming**: **ROS topics** or **MQTT brokers** (e.g., Mosquitto) for real-time telemetry.

3. **Control & Optimization**:
   - **Framework**: **ROS Control** or **Python (Control library)** for PID/MPC controllers.
   - **Optimization Tools**: **SciPy (optimize module)** or **CasADi** for trajectory planning.

4. **Database & Storage**:
   - **Time-Series DB**: **InfluxDB** or **PostgreSQL** (unspecified but common for digital twins).
   - **Metadata Storage**: **JSON/YAML** for service configurations or **ontology graphs** (e.g., RDF) for DarTwin.

5. **Visualization**:
   - **Tools**: **Python (Matplotlib, Plotly)** or **web frameworks** (e.g., **Dash**, **Streamlit**).
   - **3D Rendering**: **Blender** (for static models) or **ROS RViz** (for real-time overlays).

6. **Development Environment**:
   - **IDE**: **VS Code** or **PyCharm** (unspecified but standard for Python-based systems).
   - **Version Control**: **Git** (implied by open-source availability).
   - **Containerization**: **Docker** (unspecified but likely for reproducibility).

7. **Specialized Enablers**:
   - **ROS (Robot Operating System)**: Facilitates **sensor-actuator integration**, **service orchestration**, and **interoperability** between physical/digital components.
   - **Open-Source Licensing**: Entire system is **publicly available** under permissive licenses (e.g., MIT/BSD), ensuring **reproducibility** and **community adoption**.
   - **Interoperability Standards**: Aligns with **IEC 62264 (Enterprise-Control System Integration)** for industrial digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin systems described in the documents exhibit **ad-hoc, decentralized architectures** with **no explicit mention of multiple Digital Twin instances** or hierarchical structures. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar relies on a **single Digital Twin instance** synchronized with the physical greenhouse via **InfluxDB** (time-series database) for data storage, with **no specified synchronization frequency** or latency requirements. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses a **Raspberry Pi with sensors** for real-time data acquisition, but lacks details on **multiplicities** or **coordination mechanisms** between potential twin instances. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is not detailed enough in the provided documents to infer multiplicities or hierarchical structures. All exemplars appear to follow **centralized data collection** (via sensors or pre-recorded data) with **no decentralized twin coordination** explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems in the documents employ **ad-hoc orchestration patterns** without formalized frameworks. **GreenhouseDT** uses **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, **ActiveMQ** for message brokering (likely for asynchronous communication), and **virtual machines (VMs)** with pre-recorded data to facilitate twin-physical synchronization. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates **AutomationML-based Digital Twin** with **Microsoft Azure tools** for pipeline implementation, suggesting a **cloud-centric orchestration** with **Azure services** (e.g., IoT Hub, Stream Analytics) for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is described using the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization but lacks explicit details on **orchestration patterns**, **component integration**, or **resource management**. All exemplars avoid commercial Digital Twin frameworks, opting instead for **customized, domain-specific solutions** with **containerization/virtualization** (e.g., VMs) for deployment.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The provided Digital Twin systems exhibit **limited horizontal integration** with external systems. **GreenhouseDT** integrates with **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) but does not mention **interoperability standards** or **enterprise system dependencies**. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **Microsoft Azure tools** (e.g., IoT Hub, Stream Analytics) for cloud-based processing, implying **Azure-specific integration patterns** but no broader **open standards** (e.g., OPC UA, DDS). The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system lacks explicit details on external integrations. None of the exemplars reference **standardized data exchange protocols** (e.g., MQTT, RESTful APIs) or **interoperability with other Digital Twins**. The **ad-hoc nature** of these systems prioritizes **domain-specific solutions** over **scalable, enterprise-grade integration**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **behavioral adaptation** approach, integrating both the physical greenhouse and its digital counterpart. It utilizes **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, enabling efficient model-data synchronization. The digital twin relies on **prerecorded data** within virtual machine images for initialization, with **ActiveMQ** serving as a message broker to facilitate real-time communication between components. The **model types** include behavioral models (adaptive logic for greenhouse operations) and analytical models (likely derived from sensor data and environmental parameters). Data sources are primarily derived from embedded sensors within the greenhouse, while the **data management approach** leverages containerization (virtual machines) for reproducibility and scalability. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses an **AutomationML-based digital twin**, integrating **Microsoft Azure tools** for simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization. The system employs **geometric models** (likely CAD-based representations of indoor spaces) and **analytical models** (e.g., computational fluid dynamics for air quality simulation). Data sources include **Raspberry-Pi sensors** (e.g., CO₂, particulate matter, temperature), with data transmitted via **Azure IoT Hub** for cloud processing. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not explicitly detail its digital twin models and data components in the provided documents, but the context suggests it may use **C4 Model** for architectural visualization, implying structured data and model representations for crane dynamics and logistics. **Model relationships** in the GreenhouseDT and Air Quality Management exemplars are established via **message brokers (ActiveMQ)** and **cloud platforms (Azure)**, ensuring bidirectional synchronization between physical and digital systems. The **specific roles** of models include real-time adaptation (GreenhouseDT), predictive analytics (Air Quality Management), and simulation-driven decision-making (both).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **embedded sensors** within the physical greenhouse to monitor environmental parameters such as temperature, humidity, and light levels. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar utilizes a **Raspberry-Pi** platform equipped with a **set of sensors** (exact types unspecified but likely including CO₂, particulate matter, temperature, and humidity sensors) to measure indoor air quality. The **sampling frequencies** and **data transmission rates** are not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the use of **Raspberry-Pi** suggests low-latency, real-time data collection. The **spatial distribution** of sensors in the Air Quality Management exemplar is tailored to indoor environments, ensuring comprehensive coverage of air quality metrics. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not provide specific details about its sensing infrastructure in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical and digital systems, implying a **closed-loop validation mechanism** where discrepancies between the twin and the physical greenhouse trigger adaptive adjustments. However, explicit **model fidelity requirements**, **validation and verification methods**, or **uncertainty quantification** are not detailed. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, which inherently supports **structured model validation** through standardized data exchange. While **Microsoft Azure tools** enable simulation and prediction, the documents do not specify **error detection and correction mechanisms** or **quality assurance processes**. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar does not provide information on fidelity or validity considerations in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system is facilitated through a **Python-based Physical Crane Interface** container, which acts as the primary bridge between the physical hardware and the digital components. Data flows from the physical crane to the digital twin include **real-time sensor measurements** (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration, and state of the crane and pucks) transmitted via **proprietary controller interfaces** (unspecified protocol but assumed to be low-level hardware communication). The **Logging Application**, a Python service, continuously captures these measurements and preprocesses them before storing them in a **TimescaleDB database**.

**Data Types Transmitted**:
- Position coordinates (X, Y, Z) of the crane and pucks.
- Velocity and acceleration metrics.
- State flags (e.g., crane operational status, puck presence, error conditions).
- Timestamped events (e.g., trajectory execution start/end, anomalies).

**Communication Protocols**:
- The exact protocol is not specified, but the interface likely uses **low-latency, hardware-specific communication** (e.g., serial, USB, or Ethernet-based protocols) to ensure real-time data acquisition.

**Transmission Frequencies**:
- Not explicitly stated, but the **continuous logging** implies high-frequency sampling (likely **10–100 Hz** or higher, typical for industrial control systems).

**Event Triggers**:
- **Periodic polling** of crane controllers by the Physical Crane Interface.
- **Asynchronous triggers** for critical events (e.g., trajectory completion, sensor failures).

**Data Quality Assurance**:
- No explicit mention of validation or filtering mechanisms in the documents, though the **Continuous Validation Service** (described separately) likely includes post-logging validation of trajectory data against simulations.

**Preprocessing Steps**:
- The Logging Application likely performs **basic normalization and timestamp alignment** before ingestion into TimescaleDB, though specifics are not provided.

**Data Integration Mechanisms**:
- Data is stored in **TimescaleDB**, a time-series database optimized for high-throughput, time-ordered data. The **MQTT Broker** facilitates asynchronous communication between the Logging Application and other digital twin services (e.g., Dashboard, Simulation Service).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this Digital Twin system is orchestrated by the **Control Application**, a Python service that generates and executes commands for the physical gantry crane. Control flows involve **trajectory-based commands** derived from the **Trajectory Generator Service**, which uses a **kinematics model** to optimize crane movements with **zero-residual swinging** at the endpoint.

**Command Types**:
- **Trajectory commands**: High-level path definitions (e.g., start/end positions, velocity profiles) sent to the **Control Application**.
- **State commands**: Low-level control signals (e.g., enable/disable crane, reset controllers).

**Control Message Formats**:
- Commands are transmitted via the **Physical Crane Interface** in a **proprietary or low-level format** (unspecified), likely structured as **JSON or binary payloads** for efficiency.

**Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
- No explicit mention of authentication, but the **Continuous Validation Service** compares executed trajectories against simulated ones using **distance metrics** (e.g., Euclidean distance) to detect deviations. If thresholds are breached, operators are notified via the **Dashboard**.

**Feedback Systems**:
- The system lacks explicit feedback loops for real-time adjustments, but the **Logging Application** captures post-execution data for analysis, and the **Simulation Service** can simulate trajectories for validation.

**Conflict Resolution**:
- Not described in the documents. Assumed to rely on **priority-based or time-stamped command handling** in the Control Application.

**Command Execution Confirmation**:
- The **Physical Crane Interface** likely provides **acknowledgment signals** (e.g., success/failure flags) to the Control Application, though specifics are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure relies on an **MQTT-based messaging system** for inter-service communication, with the **MQTT Broker** acting as the central hub. The network architecture is not explicitly detailed, but the following assumptions and specifics can be inferred:

**Communication Protocols**:
- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**: Used for asynchronous, lightweight messaging between containers (e.g., Logging Application → Dashboard, Trajectory Generator → Control Application).
- **Unspecified physical layer protocols** for Physical-to-Virtual data (likely Ethernet, USB, or serial for crane controllers).

**Network Topology and Architecture**:
- **Decentralized microservices architecture**: Containers (e.g., Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service) communicate via MQTT topics, with the broker ensuring decoupled, scalable interactions.
- **No explicit mention of a service mesh or dedicated network topology**, but the C4 Model implies a **logical separation** between physical and digital components.

**Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
- Not quantified, but **low-latency requirements** are implied for real-time control (e.g., trajectory execution). MQTT’s publish-subscribe model is optimized for **high-throughput, low-latency** scenarios.
- The **Continuous Validation Service** suggests **real-time comparison** of executed vs. simulated trajectories, necessitating **sub-second latency** for validation alerts.

**Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
- No redundancy mechanisms are described. Assumed to rely on **MQTT QoS levels** (e.g., QoS 1 for at-least-once delivery) and **TimescaleDB’s durability** for data persistence.
- The **Physical Crane Interface** may include **retry logic** for failed commands, though not specified.

**Security Protocols**:
- Not mentioned. Assumed to require **MQTT TLS/SSL encryption** for broker communication and **authenticated access** to the TimescaleDB database.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin’s Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed, but the following inferences can be drawn from the context:

**Hosting Platforms**:
- **On-premise deployment**: The system is described as a **lab-scale gantry crane** with no mention of cloud or edge computing. The **Physical Crane** and its interface are co-located, implying a **single-site deployment**.

**Computational and Storage Requirements**:
- **Computational**:
  - **Trajectory Generator Service**: Requires a **kinematics model** (unspecified complexity) for real-time trajectory optimization.
  - **Simulation Service**: Likely demands **moderate computational resources** for physics simulations (e.g., crane dynamics).
  - **Continuous Validation Service**: Needs **real-time distance metric calculations** (e.g., Euclidean/Manhattan distance) between executed and simulated trajectories.
  - **Logging Application**: High **I/O throughput** for TimescaleDB ingestion.
- **Storage**:
  - **TimescaleDB**: Optimized for **time-series data**, with no specified capacity but likely **gigabytes-scale** for logged crane trajectories and sensor data.

**Scalability Mechanisms**:
- Not described. The system appears **monolithic in scope** (lab-scale), with no mention of horizontal scaling (e.g., container orchestration with Kubernetes). Vertical scaling (e.g., upgrading server resources) may be required for larger datasets.

**Deployment Models Supported**:
- **Single-node deployment**: All containers (e.g., Trajectory Generator, Dashboard) likely run on a **single machine** or a **small cluster** of interconnected servers.
- **No mention of container orchestration** (e.g., Docker Swarm, Kubernetes), suggesting a **manual or scripted deployment** approach.

**Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
- **No redundancy or failover mechanisms** are described. Reliability depends on:
  - **TimescaleDB’s durability** for data persistence.
  - **MQTT broker’s persistence** (if configured) for message queuing.
  - **Physical Crane Interface’s robustness** for hardware communication.
- **Manual operator intervention** is implied for critical failures (e.g., threshold breaches in the Continuous Validation Service).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin systems described in the documents employ ad-hoc development methodologies with a focus on containerization or virtualization technologies for deployment. For example, **GreenhouseDT** utilizes virtual machines with pre-recorded data and relies on **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, facilitating real-time twin-physical system synchronization. **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **Raspberry Pi** with onboard sensors for physical system monitoring and **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, while Microsoft Azure tools are integrated for pipeline implementation. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar, though not detailed in lifecycle stages, follows a C4 Model for architecture visualization, suggesting a structured yet domain-specific approach. Version control is implied via GitHub repositories for all exemplars, but explicit lifecycle governance (e.g., CI/CD pipelines, compliance checks) is not documented. Evolution management appears iterative, with behavioral adaptation in **GreenhouseDT** and runtime simulation/prediction capabilities in **Air Quality Management** exemplifying dynamic twin-physical system alignment.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar explicitly supports decision-making through **runtime visualization**, **simulation**, and **prediction** of indoor air quality for virus spread prevention, utilizing **Microsoft Azure** for analytics. **GreenhouseDT** enables behavioral adaptation of both the physical greenhouse and its digital twin, suggesting real-time adjustments based on sensor data (e.g., temperature, humidity) via **InfluxDB** and **ActiveMQ**. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** lacks explicit decision support details, but its cyber-physical nature implies potential for container/puck movement optimization via sensor-driven insights. User interaction methods are not detailed, but the use of **Raspberry Pi** and **AutomationML** in **Air Quality Management** suggests low-latency data processing for actionable insights. Confidence measures for recommendations are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The provided documents do not explicitly mention adherence to **industry standards** (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, IEEE P2414), **certification requirements**, or **compliance frameworks** for the Digital Twin systems. **GreenhouseDT** and **Air Quality Management** exemplars use **AutomationML** (ISO 15926-10) for digital twin representation, indicating partial standardization in **interoperability** for engineering domains. However, no mention of **OPC UA**, **ROS 2**, or other widely adopted DT standards (e.g., **OMG Digital Twin Reference Model**) is present. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** lacks standardization details. Interoperability is ad-hoc, relying on **Raspberry Pi** sensors and **Azure/InfluxDB** for data exchange without formalized protocols.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs an **ad-hoc security approach** with no explicit mention of formal data governance, privacy protection, or compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, CCPA). The system relies on **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, which lacks native encryption-by-default for sensitive data, though InfluxDB supports TLS/SSL for secure communication. **ActiveMQ** is used as a message broker, which requires manual configuration of **JMS security policies** (e.g., SASL, ACLs) for authentication and authorization. The virtual machine (VM) deployment includes **prerecorded data**, but no documentation specifies data anonymization, access controls, or consent management mechanisms. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar similarly lacks explicit security details, though it uses **Microsoft Azure tools**, which inherently include **Azure Active Directory (AAD)** for identity management and **Azure Policy** for compliance enforcement. However, the exemplar does not detail how these are implemented or whether they cover data ownership, anonymization, or sharing agreements. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is not described in the provided documents, so no security or privacy measures can be inferred. Overall, the exemplars adopt **implicit security assumptions** (e.g., containerization for isolation) but omit explicit documentation of governance, privacy safeguards, or safety-critical risk assessments.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

another.

describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
